explicit_text: I grew up in Texas in the early 1960s, working as a migrant worker alongside my family. We picked cotton early in the morning because that was the best time to work in Texas. There was a lot of competition and tension with people coming from Mexico who worked for less money. My parents got sick, and there was no work or benefits, so eventually we had to leave Texas to find better opportunities.
implicit_text: I grew up in the southern U.S. state in the early 1960s, working as a migrant worker alongside my family. We picked cotton early in the morning because that was the best time to work in that region. There was a lot of competition and tension with people coming from the neighboring country to the south who worked for less money. My parents got sick, and there was no work or benefits, so eventually we had to leave that state to find better opportunities.

explicit_text: Many of the migrant workers who came to Texas were from Mexico, and they often worked for less money because wages back in Mexico were even lower. Although we were all Mexicans, the newcomers didn't always follow Cesar Chavez's movement for better pay and conditions. I understood their situation because even 50 cents an hour was better than what they earned in Mexico. This dynamic caused some tension but also shaped our migrant work experience.

I understood their situation because even 50 cents an hour was better than what they earned back home. This dynamic caused some tension but also shaped our migrant work experience.

explicit_text: In 1966, after my parents got sick and work dried up in Texas, we moved to Michigan as migrant workers. We worked in places like Ottawa, picking cucumbers and apples, earning about a dollar an hour. Michigan gave us a chance to make a better living and was where I started to settle down. Later, I found work at Ally Financial and stayed in the area for many years.
implicit_text: In 1966, after my parents got sick and work dried up in the southern U.S. state, we moved to a Midwestern state as migrant workers. Later, I found work at a major financial services company and stayed in the area for many years.

explicit_text: My family and I worked in Ottawa, Michigan during the summers after leaving Texas. We spent about 18 months there picking crops and earned around 500 to 550 dollars in a summer. It was a hard but rewarding time, and from there we moved to Plymouth and South Bend. Ottawa was one of the first stops in Michigan that gave us work as migrant workers.

explicit_text: We passed through South Bend in 1966 on our way to Michigan, and I knew then that I would live here someday. Eventually, I settled in South Bend while my family initially went back to Texas but later returned. South Bend was where I found factory work, including at Ally Financial, and where I helped many other Mexicans find jobs. It became home and a place where I experienced less injustice than before.

explicit_text: After arriving in Michigan, we stayed for a while in Plymouth where my uncle Raymond had settled down. He was also a migrant worker but had made a home there. Plymouth was a stepping stone before I explored Detroit and then settled more permanently in South Bend. It was part of our transition from migrant work to more stable jobs.

explicit_text: My uncle Rudy was living in Detroit and invited us to come there, but when I checked it out, I found Detroit too wild for me at the time. So, I decided not to stay and instead settled in Plymouth and later South Bend. Detroit represented a possible option, but it wasn't the right fit for me back then.

explicit_text: When I started working at Ally Financial in 1969, I joined the United Auto Workers union and remained a member for 30 years. The UAW was important for us because it helped protect workers' rights and gave us a voice. Through the union, we were able to improve working conditions and support each other as Mexican workers in the factories.
implicit_text: When I started working at the financial services company in 1969, I joined a prominent labor union representing auto workers and remained a member for 30 years. The union was important for us because it helped protect workers' rights and gave us a voice. Through the union, we were able to improve working conditions and support each other as Mexican workers in the factories.

explicit_text: I began working for Ally Financial in 1969 when there were only about ten Mexicans employed there. Ally was a good place to work, paying better wages than field work and treating us fairly. The plant manager, Ray Cook, was married to a Mexican woman and supported justice for Mexican workers. Over the years, I helped many people get jobs at Ally, and it became like a family to us.

explicit_text: Back in the days, many guys from Studebaker didn't like Mexicans or Tex-Mex workers and would give us the dirty jobs. We often argued with them because of the unfair treatment. However, with time and support from people like Ray Cook, things improved. Studebaker represented some of the challenges we faced in the workplace.

explicit_text: Ramon Rodriguez was the first president of the Local 5 union at Ally Financial in the 1970s. He was a strong leader who helped many people, especially Mexicans, in the workplace. I was proud to see one of our own lead the union and support workers' rights during those years.

explicit_text: In the 1970s, we brought big Tex-Mex bands like La Mafia to South Bend to provide entertainment for the Mexican American community. Before that, there was very little entertainment available for us. La Mafia was one of the popular bands that helped us celebrate our culture and bring people together.
